behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in cells A1 through A10.
behaviors.justification: SUM formula evaluation is purely computational logic within the in-process formula engine. No I/O, external dependencies, or multiple system components are involved. A unit test with in-memory cell values can verify range parsing, summation, and arithmetic correctness. Mocking would not hide bugs in the SUM implementation itself—the test can directly verify the computed result against expected values.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1:A10 with known numeric values (mix of integers and floats). In a formula cell, enter =SUM(A1:A10). Verify the computed result equals the manual sum. Test boundary conditions: single-cell range, non-contiguous ranges if supported, and large numbers to ensure precision.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula (e.g., =B1+1 where B1 is =A1*2) produces the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: Cascaded formula resolution is entirely in-process dependency evaluation. The engine must correctly identify that B1 contains a formula (not a constant), evaluate B1's formula to get a value, then use that value in C1's evaluation. This multi-step evaluation is deterministic logic that can be tested in-memory. A unit test verifying the final computed values against expected results will catch failures in formula chaining, evaluation order, or value vs. formula-text resolution.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1=5, B1==A1*2, C1==B1+1. Verify C1 evaluates to 11. Change A1 to 10; verify B1 auto-updates to 20 and C1 to 21. Test deeper chains (4+ levels) and verify each level correctly uses computed values from its dependencies, not formula text.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1 references B1, B1 references A1) and returns a circular reference error instead of looping.
behaviors.justification: Circular dependency detection is a graph cycle-detection algorithm executed in-process before formula evaluation begins. The engine parses formulas, builds a dependency graph, and checks for cycles—all deterministic, in-memory logic. A unit test can create circular reference structures and verify that the engine returns an error before entering infinite loops. No I/O, external services, or multi-component wiring is required.
behaviors.test_description: Create direct cycle: A1==B1+1, B1==A1+1. Verify engine returns circular reference error (e.g., #CIRC or #REF) and does not hang. Test indirect cycles: A1==B1+1, B1==C1+1, C1==A1+1. Verify all cycles are detected and engine safely rejects the workbook state.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with an exact match returns the correct value from the lookup table, and returns an error when the key is not found.
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP is a deterministic in-process function that searches a table (in-memory cells) and returns a value. The function logic—key matching, column indexing, error handling—operates entirely within the formula engine. Unit tests can simulate lookup tables with in-memory cells and verify function behavior. Mocking would not hide bugs in VLOOKUP's search or column-selection logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table (Name column, Value column). Test VLOOKUP: (a) exact match returns correct value, (b) key not found returns #N/A error, (c) column index out of bounds returns error, (d) NULL or empty key handled correctly. Verify the function returns the correct column based on the column index parameter, not a fixed column.

behaviors.description: The IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")).
behaviors.justification: IF evaluation is deterministic in-process logic: evaluate the condition, select the true or false branch, evaluate the selected branch. Nesting does not introduce external dependencies. All values and branch selection happen in-process. Unit tests can verify all branches execute and return correct results.
behaviors.test_description: Create nested IF formula: =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"). Test with A1=5 (expect "low"), A1=15 (expect "medium"), A1=25 (expect "high"). Test edge cases: A1==10, A1==20, NULL values. Verify branch selection is correct at each nesting level.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with the correct separator and handles empty cells by skipping them rather than inserting blank strings.
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE is a pure string function operating on in-process cell values. The function's behavior—joining text, handling empty cells, applying separators—is deterministic and requires no I/O or external logic. Unit tests can fully verify all combinations of text, empty cells, and separators in-memory.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells with text and empty cells. Test CONCATENATE: (a) joins non-empty text correctly, (b) skips empty cells instead of inserting blanks, (c) applies separator only between non-empty values, (d) handles NULL values. Test all-empty, all-text, and mixed scenarios.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the referenced sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet reference resolution is an in-process name-resolution problem. The engine parses the sheet reference syntax (Sheet2!A1), looks up the sheet by name, and retrieves the cell value—all in-process logic. Unit tests with multiple in-memory sheets can verify parsing, sheet lookup, and cell resolution. No I/O or external services needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with Sheet1 and Sheet2. In Sheet2!A1, place value 10. In Sheet1, create formula =Sheet2!A1+1. Verify result is 11. Test error cases: reference to non-existent sheet (error), reference to non-existent cell (error). Verify references update correctly if the referenced cell value changes.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in the correct topological order.
behaviors.justification: Topological recalculation is an in-process graph algorithm that must order formula evaluation respecting dependencies. The engine builds a dependency graph and traverses it to recalculate cells in order. This is deterministic logic—unit tests can verify that each cell's dependencies are computed before the cell itself by checking intermediate values during recalculation. Bugs in topological order will cause cells to have stale or incorrect values, which unit tests will catch.
behaviors.test_description: Create dependency chain: A1=5, B1==A1*2, C1==B1+1, D1==A1+C1. Modify A1 to 10. Verify: (a) B1 recalculates to 20, (b) C1 recalculates to 21 using B1's new value (20), not old value, (c) D1 recalculates to 31 using A1=10 and C1=21. Check intermediate values to ensure dependencies are satisfied in order. Test complex graphs with multiple levels and cross-dependencies.

behaviors.description: The undo operation after editing a formula restores both the formula text and all previously computed dependent values.
behaviors.justification: Undo is an in-process state-management mechanism that must capture and restore snapshots of formula text and computed values. The test executes entirely in-memory with no file I/O—it verifies that the undo stack correctly records state before a modification and restores it when undo is called. If formula text or dependent values are not captured or restored, the in-process test will detect the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1=5, B1==A1*2 (computes to 10). Edit B1 to ==A1*3 (now computes to 15). Call undo; verify B1's formula reverts to ==A1*2 and its computed value reverts to 10. Test multi-step undo (undo twice) and redo sequences. Verify unrelated cells are not affected by undo.

behaviors.description: Saving a workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas — no data loss or precision change in floating-point results.
behaviors.justification: Save/load involves real file I/O and serialization/deserialization, which cannot be fully tested with mocks. The engine must encode cell data, formulas, and metadata into a binary (or text) format and decode it back to an identical state. Real file operations are required to catch: encoding bugs, endianness issues on different platforms, floating-point precision loss, incomplete writes, and format versioning problems. A mocked I/O test would pass even if the binary format loses data or precision.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with diverse content: integer and floating-point values, SUM/VLOOKUP/IF formulas, cross-sheet references, nested formulas. Save to disk. Load the workbook into a new engine instance. Verify: (a) all formula text is preserved exactly, (b) all cell values are identical (bit-exact for floats, no rounding errors), (c) formulas still reference correct cells, (d) dependent calculations produce same results, (e) workbook can be edited and re-saved without data loss.
